data.title: South Korea’s challenges a taste of what’s to come for global economy
data.description:
As 2025 draws to a close, three themes loom large in politics, economics and finance. The first is the unprecedented degree of uncertainty and unpredictability when it comes to the performance of economies and asset prices, amplified by US President Donald Trump’s erratic policymaking.
Who would have predicted at the start of this year that the MSCI All Country World Index, a gauge of global stocks, would stand slightly below its all-time high five weeks before Christmas? More surprisingly, who would have bet in April, when Trump launched his assault on the global trading order, that the International Monetary Fund would forecast the world economy would expand 3.2 per cent this year, on a par with its growth rate in 2024?
The second theme is affordability, the single biggest political issue in many advanced and developing economies. It is also one reason Democratic candidates triumphed in gubernatorial and mayoral elections in several US states earlier this month, especially in New York City.
Whether it is grocery and utility prices, property values or the drop in real wages, sharp increases in the cost of living are forcing policymakers to take forceful measures to rein in prices. These policies are weighing on markets, as evidenced by the increase in Japan’s 10-year bond yield to its highest level since 2008 because of concerns about the new government’s plans for fiscal stimulus to help households cope with rising prices.
The third theme is geopolitics, which is being taken more seriously by investors as its consequences for economies, industries and markets become increasingly apparent. In Bank of America’s monthly global fund manager survey on November 18, some of the respondents’ highest conviction views – the risk of an artificial intelligence-induced bubble in stocks and the bullish stance on emerging markets – are underpinned by strong geopolitical forces, especially the battle between the US and China for AI supremacy.
South Korea is experiencing all three trends simultaneously, presenting acute challenges for policymakers and providing a taste of what could be in store for other major economies. One of the most vulnerable economies to the onslaught of protectionism because of its relatively high ratio of exports as a proportion of economic output and its strong reliance on the US market, technology-driven South Korea has been one of the big surprises of 2025.

Since the start of this year, the Kospi, South Korea’s main equity index, has risen more than 65 per cent, making it the world’s best-performing major stock market. The storming rally has been powered by the rise of semiconductor stocks SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics, which together account for around a third of the Kospi’s market capitalisation.
South Korea has been one of the biggest beneficiaries of the AI boom. This far-reaching transformation is offsetting weaknesses in tech-driven economies – the US is a prime example – but also amplifying vulnerabilities.
In South Korea, the yield on the country’s 10-year bond has jumped almost half a percentage point in the past month despite the Bank of Korea (BOK) saying it remains in a monetary easing cycle. With the exception of Japan, no other leading economy has experienced a sharp rise in bond yields this year as central banks reduce interest rates in response to lower inflation.

One of the reasons for the unexpected rise in South Korean bond yields is the AI boom, which caused exports to surge 6 per cent in annualised terms in the third quarter, fuelled by demand for AI-related memory chips. According to Societe Generale, Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix alone accounted for more than 80 per cent of the returns of the Kospi in the third quarter.
Another reason for the sell-off in South Korean bonds is renewed signs of overheating in Seoul’s housing market, long a hotbed of speculation despite several rounds of cooling measures. Seoul flat prices increased at the sharpest pace since 2018 last month while rents rose at the fastest clip on record.
The deterioration in affordability, which has hit renters and lower-income households hardest, stems mainly from “lingering memories of past policy failures”, Societe Generale said. Confidence in the effectiveness of cooling measures is at rock bottom, fuelling demand in Seoul’s property market and exacerbating affordability pressures.


South Korea is also having to contend with the fallout from the escalation in geopolitical tensions. While the AI arms race has been a boon for the country’s semiconductor industry, Trump’s imposition of higher tariffs on South Korean goods has contributed to the plunge in the won to its weakest level versus the US dollar since 2009.
South Korea’s pledge to invest US$350 billion in the US in exchange for a lower levy on the nation’s imports has added to pressure on the currency. It is already under strain because of retail investors’ strong preference for US stocks and bonds despite the boom in South Korean equities. A disorderly fall in the currency, tremors in the bond market and an overheated property market in Seoul undermine the BOK’s claim that it remains in an easing cycle.
To be sure, South Korea is not alone in experiencing the challenges associated with economic uncertainty, cost of living pressures and geopolitical realignments. However, few countries are facing them all at once and at the same intensity. Given that these three themes will continue to dominate economic and market narratives in 2026, South Korea’s challenges are a taste of things to come.

data.title: Hong Kong firms pick Saudi Arabia over mainland China for expansion: HSBC survey
data.description:
Hong Kong businesses are increasingly turning to Saudi Arabia as their top choice for supply chain diversification amid changing trade conditions, according to an HSBC survey.
Nearly a fifth of Hong Kong businesses identified Saudi Arabia as their preferred market to expand production, followed by mainland China at 14 per cent, according to the survey released on Thursday. Interest was strongest among technology, media and telecommunications firms.
Favourable policies bolstered the trend, the study said, including a proposed US$1 billion fund between the Hong Kong Monetary Authority and Saudi Arabia’s sovereign wealth fund to help companies from Hong Kong and other Greater Bay Area cities expand into the Middle Eastern country. The fund would be launched “soon”, said Hong Kong Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po on Tuesday.
Last month, Chan led a 40-member delegation to Riyadh, which yielded five memorandums of understanding (MOUs) that involved collaboration in investment and technology. Maphive Technology signed an MOU with a subsidiary of Olayan Saudi Holding, part of the hospitality and events sector, while GoGoChart, a Hong Kong digital marketing start-up, struck an initial accord with an investment fund in the kingdom.

In a separate survey in June, half of the Hong Kong respondents named mainland China as their top pick, while a quarter identified South Asia – which has benefited from Hong Kong and Beijing’s efforts to forge stronger ties amid a tense US-China trade relationship.
The latest survey, which polled 6,750 decision-makers across 17 markets, found that Hong Kong firms planned to continue their diversification, with 68 per cent maintaining or planning to do so.
Nearly half of them gained more clarity over international trade policy changes compared with six months ago, while 61 per cent found it easier to understand recent trade policy changes.
As a result, optimism for international trade growth over the next two years was solid at 69 per cent, though it had decreased slightly from 74 per cent six months ago, according to the survey.
US-China tariff tensions peaked in April. A truce reached at the end of October saw a number of thorny issues, including tariffs, put on hold until August.
Hong Kong firms were more upbeat than their global peers about costs, with 22 per cent expecting tariff pressures to ease, compared with 14 per cent worldwide. Fewer firms feared steep revenue losses, with only 13 per cent anticipating declines of more than 25 per cent, down from 27 per cent six months ago.
The findings pointed to growing confidence among Hong Kong businesses in managing costs and safeguarding revenue, according to the survey.
“The proactive supply chain diversification strategy of local businesses, coupled with its enduring appeal as a highly efficient export and re-export hub, reinforces Hong Kong’s role as a major gateway in global trade,” said Frank Fang, head of commercial banking for Hong Kong and Macau at HSBC.

data.title: How the Korean game company behind Lies of P found success after struggles
data.description:
When Kim Seung-chul became Neowiz’s co-chief executive in 2021, the company was in dire need of a shift in its operations.
The company was considered one of Korea’s big three game companies in the 2000s, publishing various hit titles while games developed by its own studios achieved noticeable success.
In the 2010s, however, the domestic market shifted towards mobile titles and massively multiplayer online role-playing games (MMORPGs), and many of Neowiz’s in-house titles struggled to gain traction, leaving a handful of board game franchises to carry much of the company’s profitability for a long period.
A turning point came when the company launched its action game Lies of P in 2023 and achieved global success.


The game did not rely on what had long been considered the traditional formula for Korean games, which involved large-scale player-vs-player content, pay-to-win systems and convoluted storylines.
Instead, Lies of P focused on delivering a strong narrative through PC and console platforms and adopted a Souls-like gameplay. (“Souls-like” refers to a subgenre of action games known for their high difficulty and combat-focused play that was pioneered by FromSoftware’s Dark Souls series.)
At the G-Star 2025 game convention in Busan, Kim recalled the moment that marked “a major pivot in the company’s overall direction”, helping it focus on “building franchises that have strong intellectual property [IP]”.
“For years in this business, I felt like nothing was really accumulating, with projects following the same cycle of launching with strong revenue and then slowly fading,” said Kim, who began his career at Neowiz in 2002.
“While preparing Lies of P, we benchmarked PC and console studios like Capcom, and saw how its franchise IPs, such as Resident Evil, Monster Hunter and Street Fighter, kept growing through long-running series. That made me realise how crucial narrative is in building a franchise. Strong storytelling creates emotional attachment, and that’s what brings fans back when a sequel is released.”

Launched in September 2023, Lies of P won the grand prize at that year’s Korea Game Awards – the country’s biggest honour in games – and has sold over 3 million copies worldwide.
The new direction for Neowiz brought the company even more success that year as Sanabi, an indie game it published, became a surprise hit thanks to its solid storyline and unique gameplay.
Neowiz successfully extended the success of Lies of P by releasing its first downloadable content (DLC), Lies of P: Overture, in June. The DLC clinched two prizes at this year’s Korea Game Awards, becoming the first of its kind to do so. The company will publish Sanabi: A Haunted Day as a free DLC on November 27.


Kim said strong storytelling creates opportunities for transmedia expansion into films or animation, citing the case of Cyberpunk 2077, which expanded its universe through the Netflix series Cyberpunk: Edgerunners. Kim added that Neowiz is also exploring opportunities for film and video adaptations for Lies of P and is in contact with industry partners.
Aside from Neowiz, many other Korean game studios have been trying to expand their portfolios with narrative-driven PC and console titles in recent years, yet only a few have succeeded. Kim said the main reason is the lack of qualified manpower.
“The most difficult challenge is hiring developers with experience in single-campaign or narrative-driven games, because most developers in Korea have backgrounds in mobile MMORPGs,” he said.
“We plan to launch an aggressive hiring campaign next year because our new strategy is now on track,” he added.
“Many MMORPG studios shut down because the genre carries such a high risk of failure. Our approach offers more long-term growth and allows developers to preserve and build their capabilities over time.”
He said the company is also hiring overseas specialists in publishing.


Along with developing its own games, Neowiz is focusing on narrative-driven games in its overseas publishing business.
It signed publishing contracts with Polish game developer Zakazane in October 2024 and US-based Wolfeye Studios in January. The former is developing a narrative RPG set in the American West, while Wolfeye is working on a sci-fi RPG title that emphasises storytelling and quests.
“We are a company that actively invests in teams like these,” Kim said. “If there is a studio that wants to grow its IP over the next decade, we want to meet them and build that partnership.”
Read the full story at The Korea Times

data.title: Is naval drill a sign that India, other Quad members can look past differences?
data.description:
The Quad nations have completed a major round of naval drills, pressing ahead with their defence cooperation even as political tensions test the cohesion of the regional security bloc.
The decision to continue joint exercises reflects a “growing convergence of strategic interests” among the Quadrilateral Security Dialogue’s members and defence remaining high on the agenda despite trade and other disagreements among some of the bloc’s members, according to analysts.
Comprising Australia, India, Japan and the United States, the Quad this week concluded the Malabar exercise aimed at safeguarding a free and open order in the region’s maritime waters.
Held between November 10 and November 18 off Guam’s waters in the Northern Pacific, the naval exercise involved drills on anti-submarine and anti-air warfare, as well as counter-piracy operations.
Analysts view the annual Malabar exercises as a counterweight to China’s growing influence in the region.
Naval ships, aircraft and special forces from the Royal Australian Navy, Indian Navy, Japan Maritime Self-Defence Force and the US Navy took part in the drills, during which India showcased its self-designed stealth guided-missile frigate INS Sahyadri.

The drills were held amid uncertainties surrounding the Quad, with its multilateral focus contrasting with the current US administration’s “America First” foreign policy.
Concerns over the bloc’s unity have been raised by reports of US President Donald Trump possibly skipping the Quad leaders’ summit amid ongoing trade tensions between Washington and New Delhi.
That Malabar went ahead despite the turbulence in India-US ties shows the strength and maturity of the Quad, according to Rajeswari Pillai Rajagopalan, resident senior fellow at the Australian Strategic Policy Institute in Canberra.
“But it also reflects the institutionalised nature of these exercises that political disturbances do not have an impeding effect,” she said, adding that the drills and other bilateral military engagements were a sign of “growing convergence of strategic interests” among the Quad members.
The four countries have also conducted exercises at the bilateral and trilateral levels. India and the US took part in Exercise Tiger Triumph 2025 in April, while Australia and the US were involved in Talisman Sabre in August, with participation by India.
Alexander Vuving, a professor at the Daniel K. Inouye Asia-Pacific Centre for Security Studies in Honolulu, said the Quad’s common strategic interests in opposing China’s regional dominance could “trump the irritants” in other aspects of their relationships.


“Amid the uncertainties in economic and other domains, their shared interest in a free and open Indo-Pacific is certain,” Vuving said.
The Quad members have viewed China’s assertiveness with concern, including its naval manoeuvres in the South China Sea and significant presence along the India-China border, according to analysts.
John Bradford, executive director of the Yokosuka Council on Asia-Pacific Studies, said diplomats from the Quad nations had consistently emphasised that while the Malabar exercise involved the four countries’ navies, it was not a Quad initiative.
Involving only India and the US during the inaugural naval exercise held in 1992, Malabar preceded the creation of the Quad in 2007, the latter of which was revived in 2017.
“The diplomatic insistence that the Malabar is not a Quad event has generally been interpreted as an attempt to de-emphasise the Quad’s military aspect. However, we now see that the separation ensures that the four navies will exercise together even though political relations are strained,” Bradford said.
Ties among the Quad members at the bilateral level have come under strain due to Washington’s sweeping tariff policy, while the Quad leaders’ summit is reportedly unlikely to take place this year.
“But that doesn’t mean it is the death of the Quad,” Rajagopalan said, adding that the working and ministerial level meetings could keep “the agenda rolling”.

Noting that the year had been a “shaky” one for Quad, Bradford cited distractions arising from differences between Trump and Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi over trade and energy purchases from Russia, and Japan’s focus on domestic politics.
Japan has experienced political instability recently after the ruling Liberal Democratic Party-led coalition lost its majority in both houses of the Diet, even as it grappled with the rising cost of living and other economic issues.
Washington’s sanctions targeting Russian oil have prompted Indian refiners to pause or reduce purchases from Moscow to ensure compliance.
As host of this year’s Quad leaders’ summit, India has not managed to push through the event due to the “problematic headwinds”, according to Bradford.
Vuving said India’s close economic ties with Russia had also been a “big irritant” to its fellow Quad members Japan and Australia.
“Sometimes you cannot have the cake and eat it,” Vuving said.
While India valued its strategic autonomy, it was also aware of the importance of the Quad as a key pillar of its multilateral alliances, he added.
“There will be lots of adjustments within the Quad, but the more the great power competition intensifies, the more the Quad is important for the Quad nations.”

data.title: Fan Zhendong cements legacy at National Games after completing ‘super grand slam’
data.description:
Long regarded as a legend of the game, Fan Zhendong stands as one of the most decorated and well-rounded players in table tennis history, yet he has not stopped exploring his limits.
Following his gold medal success in the National Games men’s singles, Fan will be back in action on Thursday in the team final. With Xu Xin by his side, Fan will be up against world No 1 Wang Chuqin and another legend of the game, Ma Long.
Fan is aware of the challenges – rhythm, focus, coordination and adaptation – when moving from individual to team events.
“Today, the switch to the team event mindset was a bit slow … How to launch the attack and better enter the match during the process is excellent experience and learning for myself,” he said after the team group stage.
Playing at the top level and adapting his game to meet the gruelling demands of elite table tennis is something Fan is more than used to.

Born in 1997, Fan was affectionately dubbed by mainland media and fans as “Little Fatty” in his youth because of his build and explosive power.
It was clear from a young age that he had a special talent and it was not long before Fan started dominating the junior and then professional international circuits.
And it was only a matter of time before the Chinese table tennis national team came knocking. Despite being only 15, Fan joined the national team in 2012, adding to expectations and boosting his fame.
Those expectations were exceeded earlier in the National Games when Fan joined a prestigious list of players by completing the “super grand slam” after his dominant victory in the men’s singles final.
The super slam is a uniquely Chinese honour and has been achieved by only a few athletes who have won the Olympics, World Championships and World Cup, along with key continental and domestic titles.
Fan’s comprehensive 4-1 victory against Lin Shidong was the final domestic piece that cemented his legacy as the undisputed king of Chinese table tennis, in China and internationally. He joins a small list of champions, including the legendary Ma.
Speaking after his victory against Zhou Yu of Hebei in the round of 32 last week, Fan expressed a sense of deep honour for the opportunity to witness and take part in the multi-sports event held in Guangdong, Hong Kong and Macau this year.
“To be able to participate in this Greater Bay Area National Games and witness such an important historical moment, I feel very honoured as an athlete,” he said.
Throughout the Games, Fan has frequently discussed the importance of focus and finding his rhythm, highlighting the importance of a good start after his first match against Yu.
“The first game was crucial; winning it gave me more room to adjust,” he said post-match. “Overall, in the first two games, I struggled a bit to find my rhythm, but I played more freely later on.”
In the round of 16 against Xue Fei, Fan lost a game despite holding a substantial lead, a situation he admitted taught him a valuable lesson.
“While leading by a large margin, I took things for granted a bit, thinking I could just cruise to victory,” he said.
“But the opponent truly unleashed strong energy when falling behind. The best-of-seven format allows for one or two mistakes of this kind.”
He noted that after losing the fourth game, he quickly recalibrated his mindset.
“I made an effort to focus on the fifth game and mobilise more actively,” he said, showing the ability to accept errors and reset quickly.
In the high-stakes semi-final clash against Wang, Fan once again emphasised the need for concentration.
“Facing a high-level opponent, every small lapse in concentration can change the course of the match.
“Playing a few good points doesn’t mean you win the match, so I constantly remind myself to stay focused.”

In the final, facing the aggressive challenge of the younger Lin, Fan’s victory was hard fought.
He admitted post-match that his mindset was unstable in the first two games until he managed to reverse the deficit from 9-4 down in the first game, creating space for better play.
Reflecting on the victory and his self-evaluation, Fan concluded: “I think I did a decent job.
“I met significant difficulties in stages during every match, but I believe that the attitude and determination to face the match and yearn for victory have not wavered.”
He specifically acknowledged his opponent, noting that Lin’s overall ability was “already very strong”, creating significant threats.
It may be that Lin will one day take Fan’s crown as the king of the sport, but that would happen only with a similar level of dedication and resilience taken by “Little Fatty” to heights rarely achieved by others.

data.title: Tokyo complains over video of diplomat appearing to bow to Chinese official
data.description:
Footage of a Japanese diplomat appearing to bow to his Chinese counterpart following their talks in Beijing on Tuesday has prompted a complaint from Tokyo that it was not informed about media filming after the meeting.
It comes amid a diplomatic feud between Beijing and Tokyo over Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s remarks on Taiwan.
Tuesday’s meeting between China’s Asian affairs chief Liu Jinsong and his Japanese counterpart Masaaki Kanai ended without consensus.
The video of Liu and Kanai – with the hashtag “Japanese official bows his head while listening to the Chinese side” – was posted after the talks by Yuyuan Tantian, a social media account run by Chinese state broadcaster CCTV. It has since been deleted.

In the video clip, Kanai is seen slightly bowing while Liu speaks, with his hands in his pockets, following their talks.
Liu wears a suit in a style that was popular among student protesters during the anti-imperialist political movement of the 1920s. He also has a Chinese national emblem lapel pin on his jacket.
Tokyo has complained to Beijing over media coverage of the two officials after their meeting.
“We have raised the issue with the Chinese side regarding the press arrangements that were made without coordination with the Japanese side,” Japanese Chief Cabinet Secretary Minoru Kihara told reporters in Tokyo on Wednesday.
In response, Chinese foreign ministry spokeswoman Mao Ning said Beijing did not make arrangements with media outlets to take photos or film.
“I actually noticed that most of the reporters at the scene were from Japanese media outlets,” she added.
Kanai, who heads the Japanese foreign ministry’s Asia and Oceania bureau, made the trip to the Chinese capital on Monday after Beijing advised citizens to avoid travelling to Japan in the near term and to reconsider studying in the country, citing safety risks.
Tensions have escalated since Takaichi told a parliamentary committee on November 7 that an attack by Beijing against Taiwan could constitute a “survival-threatening situation” for Japan, and it could justify Japanese military intervention.
Beijing sees Takaichi’s statement as meddling in China’s internal affairs, and it has demanded that she retract the remarks – which she has refused to do.


On Wednesday, Kihara said Tokyo was seeking dialogue with Beijing to de-escalate the situation.
“There is no change in our policy to reduce challenges and pending issues while increasing understanding and cooperation through mutual efforts,” he was quoted as saying. “We will continue to monitor the situation closely and take appropriate action.”
During their talks, Liu told Kanai that the Japanese prime minister must retract her remarks, according to the Chinese foreign ministry.
Kanai did not speak to reporters after their meeting, while Liu said he was “very unsatisfied” with the talks, Chinese media reported.
In response to the travel alerts, Kanai “strongly” urged Beijing to take appropriate measures and said Japan’s security situation had not deteriorated, according to Japan’s foreign ministry. Kanai also asked Beijing to ensure the safety of Japanese citizens in China.
On Wednesday, a Chinese cruise ship cancelled a planned stop at Miyakojima, in Japan’s southernmost prefecture Okinawa, saying passengers had requested not to go there, according to media reports.
Beijing views Taiwan as part of China, to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including Japan and the United States, do not acknowledge the self-ruled island as an independent state but oppose any forcible attempt to change the status quo.

data.title: China tells Japan to ‘behave’ properly as it dimisses call for talks
data.description:
China has urged Tokyo to “behave” with restraint after it called for talks amid the diplomatic freeze over Prime Minister Sunae Takaichi’s comments about Taiwan.
Asked about a possible meeting between Premier Li Qiang and Tkaichi on the sidelines of the Group of 20 Summit in South Africa this weekend, foreign ministry spokeswoman Mao Ning said China had no such plans.
“There’s no arrangement for Premier Li Qiang meeting the Japanese leader. We urge Japan to behave with proper self-restraint,” Mao said.
More to follow...

data.title: Hong Kong to set up firm to speed up San Tin Technopole, boost I&T industry
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities will take the lead by setting up a company to drive the development of a new innovation hub near the border, with the base focusing on prototyping, pilot production and small-scale manufacturing as strategic bets for the future.
Secretary for Innovation, Technology and Industry Sun Dong said on Thursday that the San Tin Technopole would serve as a base for advanced manufacturing and strategic IT industries, attracting global resources and talent under a market-led approach.
He also said he expected the project to contribute HK$250 billion (US$32 billion) or more to Hong Kong’s GDP annually upon its full operation, and provide more than 300,000 full-time jobs.
“As a natural extension of the Loop, the new I&T land in the San Tin area can integrate with the comprehensive industry supply chains in the mainland cities of the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macau Greater Bay Area. It can also take up the transformation and industrialisation of research outcomes derived from the Hong Kong Park,” Sun said.
He pointed to the Hetao Shenzhen-Hong Kong Science and Technology Innovation Cooperation Zone in the Look Ma Chau Loop, which emphasises research and development (R&D).
According to the plan, the new I&T land in the technopole is divided into “three hubs and three corridors”.
The three development hubs, organised geographically from north to south, are Hetao, Chau Tau and Ki Lun.
These hubs are integrated with three corresponding industry development corridors: the life and health technology, the artificial intelligence and robotics corridor, and the microelectronics and advanced industry.
“This planning structure can cater for the pace of I&T industry development and the relevant timetable for land resumption and site formation works undertaken by works departments. The development will have five phases, from introduction to industry ecosystem expansion, city-industry hub and consolidation,” Sun said.
In terms of a development model, Sun said the technopole would follow the principle of a capable government and an efficient market to collaborate.
“Consideration can be given to the establishment of a dedicated company to take forward the development of the San Tin Technopole in collaboration with the market, thereby leveraging market resources to accelerate development and optimise development costs.”
The government aims to have the first batch of 20 hectares of I&T sites formed for development by 2026.
The technopole is part of the Northern Metropolis mega-development near the border, aligning with the 14th national five-year plan to build Hong Kong as a regional innovation hub.
It aims to transform 30,000 hectares of land near the city’s border with mainland China into a new economic engine and housing hub, accommodating a population of about 2.5 million and around 650,000 jobs.
The government has earmarked 627 hectares (1,549 acres) of land for the San Tin Technopole, including 300 hectares, an area equivalent to the Hetao across the border or 17 Hong Kong science parks, designated for innovation and technology purposes.
The area is capable of accommodating up to 7 million square metres of gross floor area for research, industry and funding activities.
The initial phase of development alone is set to cover around 158 hectares, including around 43 hectares specifically for I&T and roughly 8.5 hectares for logistics use.
Beyond its I&T focus, the San Tin Technopole is envisioned as a holistic, self-sufficient community providing as many as 54,000 flats for a new population of up to 159,000, while generating about 165,000 jobs, with 120,000 of them on I&T sites.
Key infrastructure projects are set to be completed in phases to improve the connectivity of the technopole, such as the Kwu Tung station, situated between Sheung Shui and Lok Ma Chau stops on the East Rail Line as part of the Northern Link, in 2027.
The next phase will focus on developing the technopole with residential units and major rail links.
Residents are expected to begin moving in from 2031. The Northern Link is scheduled for completion by 2034.
Hong Kong currently has another two I&T bases, including the Science Park and Cyberport, serving distinct yet complementary roles in the city’s innovation ecosystem.
The Science Park concentrates on R&D and start-up incubation, providing specialised laboratories and facilities for physical science commercialisation and advanced manufacturing.
The Cyberport, meanwhile, focuses on digital technology, fintech, and fostering the digital entrepreneurial community with a strong emphasis on software, smart living and information and communications technology services.

data.title: Hong Kong hedge fund founder buys luxury home in Shouson Hill for US$74.3 million
data.description:
A company associated with Vincent Gao Leyi, the founder of Hong Kong-based hedge fund CoreView Capital, has bought a luxury home in the city’s Southern district for about HK$579 million (US$74.3 million), according to official records.
Matrix Properties was identified as the buyer of House 6 at the upscale development No 15 Shouson Hill in one of the city’s most exclusive enclaves, Land Registry documents showed.
According to the Companies Registry, Gao serves as a director of Matrix Properties along with Jiang Yijun. Gao is also the CEO and chief investment officer of CoreView, according to his LinkedIn page.
Jiang and Bosco Consultancy, with a registered address in the British Virgin Islands, were listed as shareholders of Matrix Properties.
Based on the purchase price, the 9,414 sq ft six-bedroom unit was valued at HK$61,499 per square foot.
Gao did not immediately reply to several requests for comment on Thursday.
The CoreView CEO was formerly with the Beijing-headquartered venture capital firm Hillhouse Capital, which he joined in 2009 and where he was responsible for investments in technology, media and telecommunications. He left the firm in 2019.
The latest luxury home purchase underscores an investment upswing in this segment of the city’s property market, alongside a broader recovery in demand for residential properties.

No 15 Shouson Hill – a development of Emperor International Holdings, C C Land Holdings and CSI Properties – has 15 luxury villas, eight of which have been sold so far with a total value of about HK$4.5 billion.
Following a deal made in October last year that fetched HK$845 million, or about HK$69,991 per square foot, for House 2, Emperor in the same month put House 6, along with House 10 and House 15, for tender.
In recent months, a number of Hong Kong’s most affluent have acquired upscale homes in a series of transactions after nearly four years of decline in luxury flat prices.
Homes with areas of at least 1,076 sq ft saw prices decline by as much as 22.7 per cent since they peaked in October 2021, according to government data. So far this year, prices of these properties have inched up by 0.03 per cent.
Meanwhile, 51,361 new and lived-in homes had changed hands from January to October, according to official data. That put this year’s residential sales on track to exceed the 53,099 units sold in 2024.
Last week, the vice-chairman and executive director of Tomson Group, Charles Tong Chi-kar, bought a HK$112 million 2,335 sq ft four-bedroom home at The Legacy in Mid-Levels West, according to the Land Registry.
Hong Kong-listed Tomson Group, known for its luxurious Tomson Riviera residential project in Shanghai’s Pudong district, has its primary operations in mainland China. The firm reported a sharp rise in interim earnings for the first half of this year, largely on the back of sale proceeds from the first phase of Tomson Foresta, another major Pudong development.
Other recent transactions include the purchase of two connected units at Wheelock Properties’ Kadoorie Hill project in Kowloon by the son of former financial secretary Henry Tang Ying-yen. Sold for HK$36.4 million each, the flats were handed over on October 3.
That same month, Hysan Development sold three units at its Bamboo Grove complex in Mid-Levels Central for a total of HK$133 million. Two of them were bought by the son of company chairwoman Irene Lee Yun-lien, while the third was acquired by the wife of non-executive director Lee Chien.
In late September, Swire Pacific chairman Guy Bradley bought a 2,832 sq ft flat at 10A-10B Stanley Beach Road for HK$50 million, according to Midland Realty. Swire Pacific controls Swire Properties and Cathay Pacific Airways.
Former Hong Kong stock exchange CEO Francis Yuen Tin-fan and Lo Ka-shui, chairman of developer Great Eagle Holdings, also recently entered the luxury home market.

data.title: Fans ecstatic after Michelle Yeoh reveals she owns over 100 Labubu dolls
data.description:
Oscar-winning Malaysian actress Michelle Yeoh melted hearts on the Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon after sharing that she has more than 100 Labubu dolls, a secret number fans never saw coming.
Yeoh revealed that she has over 100 of them tucked away at home.
“Oh maybe a little more than a hundred of them,” she shared casually on the show, as if having a miniature mythical creature colony is completely normal.
Host Fallon, understandably gobsmacked, blurted out, “You have 100 Labubus?”
Without missing a beat, Yeoh confirmed she owned more than 100 Labubu dolls.
“Yeah, but they come in big and small, you know. You can’t just choose different colours,” she said.
The conversation unfolded after Fallon showed Yeoh a photo of her and her family, celebrating her birthday with a Labubu-themed cake.
“And you have Labubu cakes and this is before the trend happened.
“Michelle, you were ahead of the game. Do you love Labubus?” Fallon asked.
Yeoh admitted she did, then melted the studio with an adorable gesture: she gifted Fallon two Labubus for his daughters, featuring her initials M and Y.

Once the clip hit social media, fans could not contain themselves.
“She’s so sweet,” a user commented.
“The way she was so happy giving the mini Labubu to Jimmy,” wrote another.
A user added: “The best Labubu queen ever.”
This story was first published by The Star
